messages.content:
Intermediate and unrounded tokens dominate at both later ages,
with a slight preference for the intermediate form, reminiscent of the Fenland adolescents studied by Britain (1997, 2000) translate into Chinese
在后期年龄段，中间和未圆润的语音元素占主导地位，稍微偏好中间形式，这让人想起了Britain（1997, 2000）研究的芬兰青少年。

In contrast, Neil shows erratic behavior in childhood, with an adolescent spike
in rounded pronunciations at age 14 (see figure 7.5). From age 21 on, Neil has an
almost categorical use of a completely unrounded pronunciation. translate into Chinese
相比之下，Neil在童年时期的发音行为颇为不稳定，他在14岁青少年时期的发音出现一个圆润音的高峰（见图7.5）。从21岁开始，Neil几乎完全统一地使用了一个完全未圆润的发音。

4. Conclusions: Broader implications
of the comparison of broad A and short U
We are now in a position to compare the fate of the two northern phonemes, and we
have seen that they fare very differently, both from each other and in the speech of
the two individuals we have been following. Before summarizing these results, let
us consider Trudgill’s (1986:18) presentation of the situation, which he presents on
the basis of his own casual observations, noting that at the time of writing the situation had “not yet been studied in any systematic way.” His view of the British situation involves a consideration of the same two vowels we have been dealing with,
beginning with a presentation of how speakers of the two dialects stereotype each
other: translate into Chinese

4. 结论：比较广元音A和短元音U的更广泛含义

我们现在可以比较这两个北部音素的命运了，我们已经看到它们的发展各不相同，无论是彼此之间还是在我们一直追踪的两个个体的语言中。在总结这些结果之前，让我们考虑一下Trudgill（1986:18）对这种情况的表述，他是基于他自己的非正式观察提出的，在写作时提到这种情况“尚未以任何系统的方式进行研究。” 他对英国情况的看法涉及了我们一直在处理的同两个元音的考虑，从介绍两种方言的说话者如何彼此成见开始：

In England, “Northerners” are stereotyped by “Southerners” as saying butter etc.
as /btə/ rather than /btə/, and as saying /dns/ rather than /da:ns/. “Southerners,”
on the other hand, are stereotyped by “Northerners” as saying /da:ns/ rather than
/dns/, while the pronunciation of butter appears to be of relatively little significance and is rarely commented on. It is therefore interesting to note that Northerners moving to the South and accommodating to Southern speech usually modify
butter /btə/ to /btə/ or at least to /bətə/, but much less rarely modify /dns/ to
/da:ns/.” (Trudgill 1986:18)   translate into Chinese

在英格兰，“北方人”被“南方人”以使用/bʊtə/而非/bʌtə/来发“butter”等单词的方式来加以刻板印象，而且用/dʊns/来发音，而非/da:ns/。另一方面，“南方人”被“北方人”以使用/da:ns/而非/dʊns/来发音的方式来加以刻板印象，而“butter”的发音似乎相对无关紧要，很少有人评论。因此，值得注意的是，搬到南方并适应南方语言的“北方人”通常会把butter的发音从/bʊtə/修改为/bʌtə/，或至少修改为/bətə/，但把/dʊns/修改为/da:ns/的情况则要少得多。（Trudgill 1986:18）

Taken as a prediction of what Neil and Nicholas would do, Trudgill’s characterization is remarkably accurate in the case of Nicholas. Nicholas modifies short A little
if at all, but he has massively altered his phonetics in the pronunciation of short U.
Trudgill interprets this differential as a consequence of the differential social meaning
of the two vowels as explained in the passage above: short A is of social (regional)
significance to northerners but not short-U. Trudgill (1986:18) thus goes on to say,
“Many Northerners, it seems, would rather drop dead than say /da:ns/, the stereotype
that this is a Southern form is again too strong” (emphasis in the original).
In other words, short-U, not being salient to northern speakers, is available for
phonetic adjustment at no social cost. Since both Neil and Nicholas have largely
abandoned their northern short-U phonetics, Trudgill’s (1986) prediction seems
right on target in this case. Neil has made a more radical change; Nicholas, a more
modest adjustment in his predominant use of an intermediate form; but both have
indeed changed. We are left with the mystery of how these speakers have succeeded
in unmerging their previous merged short-U category. It is one thing to alter a
phonetic target; it is a linguistically and cognitively more complex operation to
differentially alter different lexical items originally merged in one category. A wordby-word learning process such as that suggested by Trudgill would seem the most
likely path, yet we do not have evidence of this in the data we have been able to
examine here. Indeed, some variation has been noted among individual tokens of
the same word. translate into Chinese

作为对Neil和Nicholas行为的预测，Trudgill的描述在Nicholas案例中显得非常准确。Nicholas对短元音A的改变很小，如果有的话，但他在短元音U的发音中作出了巨大的调整。Trudgill将这种差异解释为两个元音不同的社会意义的后果，正如上述摘要中解释的那样：对北方人来说，短元音A具有社会（地区）意义，但短元音U则不具备。Trudgill（1986:18）继而说道，“看来，很多北方人宁愿死也不会说/da:ns/，因为这是一种强烈的南方形式的刻板印象”（原文中有强调）。

换句话说，短元音U对北方人来说并不显著，所以可以在不承受社会成本的情况下进行语音调整。由于Neil和Nicholas都在很大程度上放弃了他们的北方短U语音特征，Trudgill（1986）的预测在这个案例中看起来是正确的。Neil进行了更激进的变化；而Nicholas则在他主要使用中间形式的情况下做了较为适度的调整；但两者的确都改变了。我们还不知道这些说话者是如何成功地将他们之前合并的短元音U类别分开的。改变一个语音目标是一回事，而将原本在一个类别中合并的不同词汇项进行差异性改变则是一个语言学上和认知上更为复杂的操作。如Trudgill所暗示的逐字学习过程似乎是最可能的途径，然而我们在这里能够检查的数据中并没有这方面的证据。实际上，在同一个词的不同语音元素中也注意到了一些变化。

As far as Trudgill’s (1986) prediction for short-A is concerned, Nicholas, but
not Neil, may qualify as one of the “many Northerners” who would drop dead rather
than say /da:ns/. Neil, however, seems to have adopted this pattern since he went
to London at about age 17. In the case of short A, it would be difficult to imagine
the learning process involved in acquiring the new pattern as anything other than
the transfer of individual lexical items into the class in which a northern speaker
would already have palm, can’t, father, and so on. Once again, no hypercorrections
were found in Neil’s speech for short-A.12 One could claim that because Neil is
clearly not at pains to affirm his northern identity, his identity is not invested in
this northern feature and thus giving it up has had no significance for him. And
yet, Neil’s one token of short-A at age 35 occurs in his excited and spontaneous
reaction to a near mishap on stage, speaking to a local Shetlander [example (1)
above]. translate into Chinese

就Trudgill（1986）关于短元音A的预测而言，Nicholas可能可以算作“许多北方人”之一，他们宁愿死也不会说/da:ns/。然而，Neil似乎在大约17岁左右去到伦敦后就采用了这种模式。对于短元音A来说，在习得新模式的学习过程中，很难想象除了将各个词汇项转移进一个北方说话者可能已经有palm、can't、father等词汇的类别之外的其他方式。再次地，Neil的短元音A语音中没有发现任何过度校正的例子。有人可能会声称，因为Neil显然并不费心去确认他的北方身份，他的身份并没有投注在这一北方特征上，因此放弃这一特征对他来说并没有什么意义。然而，在Neil 35岁时的一个短元音A用例中，发生在他激动而自发地反应于舞台上的一次小意外，对一个当地的设得兰岛人说话时[以上例子（1）]。

In the case of Nicholas, an emotional factor appears with short-U. By age 35,
Nicholas has largely abandoned his original short-U phonetics, but we suddenly hear
two fully rounded short-U tokens when he is asked how he felt when, as a child, he
came to understand that his baby brother was deaf. With a choked voice and tears in
his eyes, Nicholas says, translate into Chinese
在Nicholas的案例中，短元音U似乎伴随着情感因素。到了35岁，Nicholas已经在很大程度上放弃了他原来的短元音U的发音特征，但当被问及他小时候意识到他的弟弟是聋人时有何感受时，我们突然听到他说出了两个完全圆润的短元音U。Nicholas带着哽咽的声音和眼中的泪水说道：

我不认为Neil和Nicholas在这些特定情感时刻发生北方形式的偶然，并且我相信我们距离完全理解我认为所涉及的三个问题还很远：
1. 不同的音系或音标特征在多大程度上携带不同的社会意义；
2. 说话者对这种音标变异有多少意识；
3. 说话者在多大程度上能够控制他们的语言产出中这种变异。

One issue, however, seems to be resolved by the speech production of Nicholas
and Neil over this 28-year-span of their lives, and that is the question posed at the
beginning of this chapter in connection with the glottal stop in Glasgow: how plausible is it that speakers might alter their phonetics over the course of their lives? And
secondarily, what implications can we draw in terms of age grading versus apparent
time interpretations of synchronic distributions? It is clear that these two speakers
have made some significant phonetic, and possibly phonemic, alterations to their
speech after adolescence. Such data confirm the plausibility of age grading as a viable interpretation but tell us little about the probability that such an interpretation
would be the correct one in any particular case.
Both Nicholas and Neil have had unusual personal histories, and the fact that
they have made significant alterations to the linguistic systems of their childhood
and early adolescent days is an important psycholinguistic observation on the possibilities and the limits of individual malleability. But the social fact is that most people
do not have such unique personal histories. The individual achievements of Neil and
Nicholas are the products of their special social histories, and they are, in spite of
their unique personalities, recognizable social types. Neil and Nicholas represent
remarkable trajectories of individual linguistic enterprise, and they remind us that
our generalizations about the speech community are not intended to set limits on what
any individual can do in the way of language learning. Even in considering the phonetic changes they have made, we must remember that neither has somehow made
himself over linguistically, so that he would in any circumstances be taken as a speaker
of a different dialect. We must also remember that these two men represent exceptional individuals that can only be seen as exceptional against a backdrop of the stable
social fact that most individuals do not alter their phonetic systems over their lifetimes. Brink and Lund (1975, 1979) show remarkable stability among the Danish
speakers they investigated over many decades, and Sankoff et al. (2002) also show
that change is the exception rather than the rule in a 24-year panel study of a cohort
of Montreal French speakers. translate into Chinese

然而，一个问题通过Nicholas和Neil在他们生活中这28年跨度的语言产出似乎已经解决了，那就是本章开头提到的问题，关于格拉斯哥声门塞音：说话者在其一生中可能改变他们的语音特征这一点有多可信？其次，我们可以从同步分布中的年龄分级与表观时间解释方面得出什么含义？很明显，这两位说话者在青少年之后对他们的语音做出了一些重要的，并且可能是音位的改变。这样的数据证实了年龄分级作为一个可行的解释的可能性，但却告诉我们很少有关于这种解释在任何特定情况下是正确的可能性。

Nicholas和Neil都有不寻常的个人历史，他们对儿时和早年青春时期语言系统所做的重大改变是一个重要的心理语言学观察，展示了个体可塑性的可能性和限制。但社会事实是，大部分人并没有这样独特的个人历史。Neil和Nicholas的个人成就是他们特殊社会历史的产物，而且尽管他们有独一无二的个性，他们仍然是可以识别的社会类型。Neil和Nicholas代表了个人语言企业的非凡轨迹，他们提醒我们，我们关于语言社区的概括并不是为了限制任何个人在语言学习方面能做什么。即使考虑到他们所做的语音改变，我们也必须记住，他们没有以某种方式在语言上完全改造自己，以至于在任何情况下都被视为不同方言的说话者。我们还必须记住，这两个男人代表着特殊的个体，只有在大多数个体在其一生中不会改变他们的语音系统这一稳定的社会事实的背景下，才能被视为特例。Brink和Lund（1975，1979）展示了他们调查的丹麦说话者在许多年里的显著稳定性，而Sankoff等人（2002）也在对蒙特利尔法语说话者群体进行的长达24年的面板研究中显示，变化是例外而非规则。

What are the implications of the present findings for the apparent time interpretation of age-graded data? In the 35 years since quantitative methodology was developed for the study of language change in progress, the few longitudinal studies
have all shown a combination of age grading and real-time effects. Labov (1994)
reviews four real-time replications of earlier work: Hermann’s restudy of Gauchat’s
research in Charmey, Switzerland; Fowler’s replication of Labov’s department store
study in New York City; and new work by Cedergren and Trudgill on Panama City
and Norwich, respectively. In all cases, results showed real-time change in the community on a majority of the variables that had been studied initially, but they also
showed some changes that looked as if individuals were modifying some of their
 phonological patterns as they grew older, that is, age grading. My own assessment is
that apparent time, as well as the critical period for language acquisition, are both
hypotheses well grounded in a substantial body of research. In both cases, they can
guide longitudinal research, designed to refine and deepen our understanding of
exactly how language change and variation plays out over the life course of individuals and of speech communities. translate into Chinese

对于通过表观时间解释年龄分级数据的现象，目前的发现有什么含义？在发展定量研究方法来研究正在进行的语言变化35年间，少数纵向研究都显示了年龄分级和实时效应的结合。Labov（1994）回顾了早期研究的四个实时重复工作：Hermann对Gauchat在瑞士Charmey的研究的再研究；Fowler对Labov在纽约市百货公司的研究的复制；以及Cedergren和Trudgill分别对巴拿马城和诺里奇的新工作。在所有案例中，结果显示大多数最初研究的变量在社区中发生了实时变化，但同时也显示了一些个体好像在他们变老时修正了一些自己的语音模式，也就是年龄分级。我自己的评估是，表观时间以及语言习得的关键期都是基于大量研究的假设。在这两种情况下，他们都可以指导纵向研究，旨在完善和加深我们对于语言变化和变异如何在个体和语言社区的一生中发挥作用的理解。

Notes
Many thanks to <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>, David Britain, Jack Chambers, Bill Labov, and Peter Trudgill
for very helpful advice at several stages in the work reported in this chapter.
1. Michael Apted’s remarkable and unique documentary, “Seven Up,” being in the public
domain, is available to readers who would like to listen for themselves to the examples cited
in this chapter. Though for reasons of length it was not possible to include my actual coding
of the data as an appendix, I would be glad to furnish this document to interested readers
(<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS>).
2. The actual phonetics of broad A may vary according to region. David Britain (personal communication) notes that the [ɑ:] form is only “truly back” in RP, and in London and
surrounding counties, whereas “many southern dialects have a much fronter [a:] as the dominant one.” There is, however, no doubt about the basic southern pattern that involves broad
A as a separate phoneme, whatever the variation in its phonetic instantiation.
3. Neil here uses a short-A pronunciation of moustache. Though Jespersen (1949) cites
only the broad-A pronunciation for this word, both Peter Trudgill and David Britain noted
(in personal communications, for which I am very grateful) that moustache is now variable
for southern speakers. Thus it does not constitute a token that can be used as part of the pool
of southern broad-A words in measuring the degree to which Neil has shifted. Excluding
moustache gives Neil at 35 a “broadness” score of 7.5/9 (or .83) rather than the 8.5/10 (or
.85) that he would have scored if moustache had been included in the data base.
4. Trudgill (personal communication) notes that in the Shetlands, “Pam and palm, lagger
and lager etc. are pronounced the same.” Thus while the Shetlands does not share the northern pattern with respect to short-A versus broad-A words, it certainly does not have the southern
broad-A category and thus does not constitute a milieu in which any recent adoption by Neil
of southern broad A could be reinforced.
5. Harris (1996) notes that this picture is somewhat oversimplified, but it is adequate
for our purposes of characterizing the major differences between northern (Harris’s Type I)
and southern (Type II) dialects.
6. According to Harris (1996:13), wedge today “is typical of most North American
English but is now recessive in southern English. The vowel tends to be nearer low central ɐ
in Standard English English . . . with even fronter reflexes being found in vernacular southern English.” And Macaulay (1988) notes that RP much tends to be homophonous with match,
an observation with which I concur on the basis of listening to the upper-class southern speakers
in Apted’s films.
7. There are, however, some northern dialects in which a lengthened [u:] pronunciation
is used in book, look, cook, hook, and so on, and thus these words are not homophonous with
buck, luck, and so on (David Britain, personal communication).
8. I transcribed all of the speech from both speakers from the film, then selected the
short-U tokens, going back to code them (on two widely separated occasions) for the degree
of rounding. In selecting tokens, I omitted any that occurred in fully unstressed syllables since
reduction to shwa in unstressed syllables made moot any assessment of vowel quality. Thus, prefixes like un- or function words like but were not included unless they were pronounced
with some degree of stress.
9. According to Wells (1982:352), intermediate forms are also typical of “near RP
Northern.” Although there may have been some speakers of this variety at the northern prep
school Nicholas was attending as a boarder at age 14, there is no trace of intermediate forms
in his own speech at this age. I therefore conclude that the intermediate forms in his speech at
21 and 28 years of age are a result of his exposure to southern dialects as of age 16.
10. Some Anglophone L2 speakers of French I have observed in Montreal pronounce
the vowel of both le and la in such a way as to blur the vowel quality distinction, a solution
they may find handy when not quite certain which gender to assign.
11. Throughout my childhood and adolescence, I had intimate and regular contact with
my maternal grandmother, Margery Gill, an unreconstructed northern dialect speaker who
went through life with variable hypercorrection of short-U words. Born in the town of Eccles,
just outside Manchester, she emigrated to Canada at the age of 25 and remained in Canada,
without a return visit, until her death more than 50 years later. Her speech was full of sporadic hypercorrections. I remember hearing [pt] for put and both [bk] and [bu:k] for book
(see note 6 above), as well as many others. Her strategy appeared to be to avoid the hated [U]
sound at all costs. But it was only as a grown woman, many years after my grandmother had
died, that I figured out that what appeared to me to be an exotic word in her vocabulary was
in fact an ordinary lexical item that we shared. A professional seamstress, my grandmother
frequently had occasion to refer to an item I called a snap, often used in sewing instead of a
button. She called it what I heard as press-stood, and it took me years to make the stud~stood
connection.
12. My years of listening to American productions of Gilbert and Sullivan have provided ample evidence of hypercorrection of short A. An American chorus may render a creditable broad-A pronunciation of, for example, class in “Bow, bow ye lower middle classes”
and yet not restrain itself from hypercorrectly inserting the same vowel in, say, “at classical
Monday Pops.” translate into Chinese

注释
非常感谢Charles Boberg、David Britain、Jack Chambers、Bill Labov和Peter Trudgill在本章报告的工作的几个阶段提供的非常有帮助的建议。

1. Michael Apted的非凡且独一无二的纪录片《七年级》处于公共领域，愿意自行收听本章提到的例子的读者可以获取该片。尽管出于篇幅原因，不可能将我的实际编码数据作为附录包含在内，但我愿意将此文件提供给有兴趣的读者（<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS>）。

2. 广元音A的实际语音可能因地区而异。David Britain（个人交流）指出，[ɑ:]形式只有在RP中以及在伦敦及其周边县份才是“真正后位”的，而“许多南方方言有一个更靠前的[a:]作为主要的形式。” 然而，对于涉及广元音A的基本南部模式，无论在其语音实现中有何种变化，都无可置疑。

3. 在这里，Neil使用了短元音A发出moustache的发音。尽管Jespersen（1949）只列出了这个词的广元音A发音，但Peter Trudgill和David Britain都指出（我对此非常感激的个人通信中），对于南方说话者来说，moustache现在是可变的。因此，它不构成一个可以用作测量Neil改变程度的南方广元音A词汇的标记。除去moustache，35岁时的Neil的“广度”得分是7.5/9（或.83），而不是如果moustache被包括在数据库中他将得到的8.5/10（或.85）的得分。

4. Trudgill（个人交流）指出，在设得兰群岛，“Pam和palm、lagger和lager等发音相同。”因此，尽管设得兰群岛在短元音A和广元音A词汇方面没有北方模式，但它肯定没有南方的广元音A类别，因此不构成一个环境，Neil最近采纳的南方广元音A在其中得到强化。

5. Harris（1996）指出，这种图景有些简化了，但它足以用于我们描述北方（哈里斯的I型）和南方（II型）方言之间的主要差异的目的。

6. 根据Harris（1996:13），楔形元音今天“在大多数北美英语中是典型的，但在南部英语中现在是退化的。在标准英语中，该元音倾向于更接近低中央的ɐ...在南部英语口语中甚至可以发现更靠前的反射。”Macaulay（1988）还指出，RP中的much往往与match同音，基于对上流社会南方说话者在Apted电影中的听力观察，我对此也表示赞同。

7. 然而，也有一些北方方言在book、look、cook、hook等词中使用长度化的[u:]发音，因此这些词不会与buck、luck等同音（David Britain，个人交流）。

8. 我从电影中转录了两位说话者的所有语言，然后选择了短元音U的发音，在两个相隔很远的时机回过头去为其圆润程度编码。在选择标记时，我省略了任何出现在完全非重音音节的标记，因为非重音音节中减弱为牙音的情况使得对元音质量的评估变得无关紧要。因此，前缀un-或像but这样的虚词除非是带有一定程度的重音发音，否则不被包括在内。

9. 根据Wells（1982:352），中间形式也是“近RP的北方”典型的。尽管在14岁时作为寄宿生在北方预备学校就读的Nicholas可能遇到了这种变体的一些说话者，但在他那个年龄时自己的语言中并没有中间形式的迹象。因此，我得出结论，他在21岁和28岁时语言中的中间形式是由于他从16岁起接触南方方言的结果。

10. 我在蒙特利尔观察到的一些以英语为第二语言的法语学习者会以这样的方式发出le和la的元音，以至于混淆了元音质量的区分，这可能是当他们不太确定要分配哪个性别时找到的一个方便的解决办法。

11. 在我的整个童年和青春期，我与我的外祖母Margery Gill有密切且定期的接触，她是一个未经修改的北方方言说话者，她一生中不定期地过度校正短元音U词汇。她出生在曼彻斯特郊外的埃克尔斯镇，25岁时移民到加拿大，在不回国的情况下在那里度过了50多年的余生。她的语言中充满了零星的过度校正。我记得听到[pʌt]代替put，和[bʌk]以及[bu:k] 代替book（见上面的注释6），以及许多其他的例子。她的策略似乎是不惜一切代价避免讨厌的[U]音。但直到我长大后，许多年过去了，我的外祖母去世后，我才发现她词汇中看起来很奇特的一个词实际上是我们共有的一个普通词汇项。作为一个专业的裁缝，我的外祖母经常有机会提到一个我称之为snap的物品，经常用于缝纫以代替纽扣。她称之为我听起来的press-stood，我花了很多年才明白stud~stood之间的联系。

12. 我多年来收听美国制作的Gilbert和Sullivan歌剧，发现短元音A的过度校正证据充分。一个美国合唱团可能会为“Bow, bow ye lower middle classes”中的 class 这个例子提供一个可信赖的广元音A发音，但却不能克制自己在“at classical Monday Pops”中过度校正插入相同的元音。
